chapters.body:
The [[port-actual|Port of Port-Actual]] was not, in any meaningful sense, a single port. By the time [[elzbieta|Elzbieta Vorn]] arrived on the 14th of Drymonth aboard the *Catechism of Tides*—a mid-displacement packet vessel flagged alternately to four jurisdictions depending on which was least inconvenient at any given moment—the port had achieved the administrative character of a small nation-state operating inside the body of a larger one, its [[compact|Compact authorities]] maintaining seventeen separate arrival counters and an unknown number of informal ones, staffed by officials whose authority derived from documents no one had verified in living memory.

She had been told, by the travel writer [[sorekin|Benedikt Sorekin]], whose *Annotated Itineraries of the Concordant Fringe* she had read three times on the voyage and which she now carried folded open to page 342 like a talisman, that there were essentially two ways to clear arrival at [[port-actual|Port-Actual]]: through the [[meridian|Meridian Gate]], which processed passengers of documented or at least guessable origin, or through the [[estuarial|Estuarial Wickets]], which processed everyone else—a category whose breadth, Sorekin noted with his characteristic mix of precision and despair, had expanded considerably since the [[compact|Third Compact]] to include persons of undocumented origin, persons of disputed origin, persons whose origin was known but whose documentation had been rendered legally non-operative by one of the eleven Clarifying Instruments issued since the Compact's ratification, and persons who had simply, as Sorekin put it with admirable compression, *"arrived."*

===The Wickets===

Elzbieta was a person who had arrived. This was not a legal determination—she possessed a passport, or rather the object that functioned as her passport, issued by the [[srb|Semiperipheral Registry Bureau]] of the city of [[verevka|Verevka]] and technically valid in all signatory territories of the [[compact|Old Compact]], of which [[port-actual|Port-Actual]] was not one, having acceded instead to the [[compact|Third Compact]] under circumstances still contested in three separate international tribunals. The problem, as the *Catechism of Tides'* supercargo [[okonkwo|Maris Okonkwo]] had explained to her during their final evening at sea, was not her documents per se. The problem was the *category* those documents implied: a person whose native city maintained active diplomatic ties with exactly the parties the Port Authority was, at present, conducting quiet proceedings against.

The [[estuarial|Estuarial Wickets]] were housed in a long corrugated shed that smelled of brine, wet rope, and something Elzbieta could only identify as institutional optimism—the particular smell of forms being filled in, of categories being applied to people, of the slow grinding work of a bureaucracy that had not yet given up on legibility. The queue moved with the pace of a geological process. A woman two ahead of her was explaining her situation in what sounded like four languages simultaneously; the official, a young man of concentrated professional blankness, was taking notes on a form whose sections bore no apparent relationship to the information being offered. Beside him sat a second official doing nothing at all but watching, in the manner of someone whose function was entirely to witness.

===On the Categories of Arrival===

*[[ed|Ed. note]]: The taxonomy of arrival categories at [[port-actual|Port-Actual]] during the early Compact period is itself a substantial scholarly problem. The Port Authority's own records, partially digitized and accessible via the [[archives|Concordant Archives]] in [[verevka|Verevka]], list between eleven and twenty-three distinct arrival classifications depending on which internal memo one consults; the Compact Secretariat maintained its own parallel taxonomy of nine categories which overlapped imperfectly with the Port's; and the informal categories in use among porters, accommodation touts, and [[fixer|licensed fixers]] constituted yet a third system, purely practical, whose organizing principle was not origin or documentation but the amount of money a given arrival was likely to spend before they were absorbed by the city.*

Elzbieta spent three hours in the Estuarial shed and emerged, finally, with a document stamped **PROVISIONAL PRESENCE (CATEGORY FOUR-DELTA)** and an instruction slip directing her to report to the [[bureau|Bureau of Ongoing Determination]] within fourteen days to clarify her situation, or have it clarified for her. Outside, the city waited: dense, salt-corroded, loud with the argument of a dozen traffic systems, its skyline a palimpsest of several centuries of aesthetic conviction none of which had fully overwritten its predecessor, smelling even from here of cooking oil and construction and the endless activity of a place that had not, in living memory, stopped.

She had come here to find someone. She did not yet know—could not know, at this stage—that the city would find her first, that it would find her in the way cities always find the people who come looking for things inside them: by making itself the thing that needed finding, by installing itself between her and every other answer until she understood that [[port-actual|Port-Actual]] was not a location but a *procedure*, not a place one arrived at but a process one underwent, slowly, with significant paperwork, and not always to one's own satisfaction.
